topics.description: Where do you put your scripts? Learn the basics of linking JS to HTML and the difference between internal and external scripts.
What is the difference between let, const, and var? This quiz covers primitives like Strings, Numbers, and Booleans.
Math operations, comparison operators, and the difference between == and ===. Essential for any Junior developer.
Learning how to control the flow of your program using if, else if, else, and basic switch statements.
For, While, and Do-While. Learn how to repeat actions without repeating your code.
How to declare a function, pass parameters, and return values. Covers both regular functions and an introduction to Arrow functions.
Creating arrays, accessing elements by index, and understanding the length property.
A set of questions designed to test a user's knowledge of how objects work. Covers key-value pairs and dot notation vs bracket notation.
How to manipulate text using slice, replace, toUpperCase, and search methods.
GetElementById, querySelector, and other ways to find your HTML in JavaScript. Crucial for web interactivity.
Click, input, and submit. Learn how to make your buttons actually do something when a user clicks them.
The most used methods in modern development. Learn how to transform and filter lists of data easily.
Why can you call some functions before they are defined? Understanding global vs local scope.
Backticks and string interpolation. Making multi-line strings and variables inside text much easier.
A quick way to extract data from arrays and objects into separate variables.
How they differ from regular functions and when you should (or shouldn't) use them.
Introduction to asynchronous code. What is .then(), .catch(), and why do we need them for API calls?
Making asynchronous code look synchronous. The modern way to handle data fetching.
How to get data from a real server and display it on your page.
Parsing and stringifying data. Understanding the standard format for web communication.
Don't let your app crash! Learn how to gracefully handle errors when things go wrong.
How to save simple data in the user's browser so it stays there even after a page refresh.
Using console.log effectively and an introduction to the 'Sources' tab in DevTools.
Breaking your code into multiple files to keep your project organized.
Naming variables correctly, keeping functions small, and writing code that other humans can read.
